content: Universal Health Coverage (UHC) is defined by WHO as ensuring all people have access to needed health services of sufficient quality without suffering financial hardship. It encompasses three dimensions: population coverage (who is covered), service coverage (which services are included), and cost coverage (what proportion of costs are paid collectively). UHC is measured through service coverage indices and catastrophic health expenditure rates. The Sustainable Development Goal target 3.8 aims to achieve UHC by 2030, including financial risk protection and access to quality essential healthcare services.

Achieving UHC requires progressive implementation strategies: (1) Expand mandatory prepayment mechanisms (taxation or insurance) to pool risks across populations; (2) Prioritize essential service packages based on burden of disease and cost-effectiveness; (3) Eliminate user fees at point of service for priority interventions; (4) Strengthen primary care as foundation for service delivery; (5) Implement strategic purchasing to improve efficiency. Countries like Thailand achieved near-universal coverage within a decade through tax-funded schemes covering outpatient, inpatient, and prevention. Evidence shows UHC reduces poverty, improves health outcomes, and increases economic productivity.

UHC progress is assessed through the Universal Health Coverage Service Coverage Index (SCI), which tracks 14 tracer indicators across four domains: reproductive/maternal/newborn/child health (family planning, antenatal care, immunization); infectious diseases (HIV, TB, malaria treatment); non-communicable diseases (hypertension treatment, cancer screening); and health system capacity (hospital beds, health workers). Financial protection is measured by incidence of catastrophic health expenditure (>10% household income) and impoverishing health spending. Current global SCI averages 67/100, with wide variation between high-income (80+) and low-income (<50) countries.
